title: What Makes a Good Trader? On the Role of Intuition and Reflection on Trader Performance
abstract: Using laboratory experiments, we provide evidence on three factors influencing trader performance: fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and theory of mind (ToM). Fluid intelligence provides traders with computational skills necessary to draw a statistical inference. Cognitive reflection helps traders avoid behavioral biases and thereby extract signals from market orders and update their prior beliefs accordingly. ToM describes the degree to which traders correctly assess the informational content of orders. We show that cognitive reflection and ToM are complementary because traders benefit from understanding signals’ quality only if they are capable of processing these signals.

article.introduction:
The cornerstone models of financebuild on the assumption that a representative economic agent acts rationally (e.g., Markowitz (1952), Sharpe (1964), Samuelson (1970)). However, the rationality assumption, which requires that individuals correctly apply and carry out mathematical and statistical methods, has been challenged by behavioral finance studies that find a large degree of variability in individuals’ ability to solve complex problems. When presented with such problems, most individuals tend to rely on simple heuristics instead of performing the requisite calculations (e.g., see Thaler (1993,2005), Barberis and Thaler (2003), Shefrin (2007) for surveys).
The heterogeneity in individuals’ cognitive capacities suggests that we may observe significant differences in their financial decisions. Broader access to the stock market coupled with increasing complexity of the financial environment (e.g., large number of sophisticated financial instruments, interconnectedness of global markets, etc.) thus renders understanding of the relationship between individuals’ cognitive capacities and financial decisions of great practical importance.
Different factors influencing trader performance have been studied in isolation in different strands of the literature using various types of data (archival, experimental, and neuroimaging) and various measures of performance, such as forecasting skill (Bruguier, Quartz, and Bossaerts (2010); BQB henceforth) or stock-picking skill (Grinblatt, Keloharju, and Linnainmaa (2012)). In this paper, we assess the cognitive underpinnings of trading decisions using a unified market setting. To do so, we employ an experimental asset market in which we control various aspects of the trading environment (see Bossaerts (2009), Frydman et al. (2014), Noussair and Tucker (2014)). Specifically, we manage the flow of information into the market so that the effect of an individual's skill is not confounded with that of insider trading. This is the case because the experimental method allows us to randomize the distribution of information across traders so that traders exhibiting high levels of specific skills are, on average, just as informed as other traders. This market setting allows us to control the flow of private information by allocating a specific signal regarding the true value of the traded asset to each trader (see Plott and Sunder (1988); PS henceforth).
Our experimental methodology also allows us to collect a large set of characteristics on individual traders. Controlling for an extensive number of individual-level characteristics (such as financial literacy, personality traits, educational background, and risk attitudes) in addition to the flow of information in the market ensures the robustness of the effect of our conjectured predictors.
We posit that traders’ ability to learn the true value of an asset using both private information and market orders is a key to traders’ success. All else equal, a more informed trader will ultimately post more profitable orders, which results in higher earnings. In the extreme, a trader who knows an asset's true value will be assured of earning positive trading profits by systematically buying below and selling above the true value. Thus, to ensure positive trading profits, a trader should attempt to discover the asset's true value. We argue that learning the true value of an asset requires three fundamental skills, namely, the ability to conduct statistical inference, avoid behavioral biases, and assess the informational content of market signals.
Statistical inference based on available signals is crucial for traders to learn the true value of an asset. Such calculations require “fluid intelligence,” which is defined as one's ability to conduct mental simulations and engage in abstract reasoning (e.g., Mackintosh (2011)). In short, fluid intelligence is the computational capacity in which one needs to undertake mental calculations.
In addition to fluid intelligence, “cognitive reflection” is a crucial skill for traders to learn true asset value. Cognitive reflection is defined as one's ability to engage in effortful reasoning (e.g., Stanovich (2009)) and is measured using the cognitive reflection test (CRT), which consists of questions that have an intuitive but incorrect answer (e.g., “A bat and a ball cost $1.10 in total. The bat costs a dollar more than the ball. How much does the ball cost?” Frederick (2005)). Upon reflection, one may disregard the intuitive answer (“10¢”) in favor of the correct one (“5¢”). Cognitive reflection adds to fluid intelligence because it helps individuals avoid commonly observed heuristics and biases (e.g., Oechssler, Roider, and Schmitz (2009), Campitelli and Labollita (2010), Hoppe and Kusterer (2011), Toplak, West, and Stanovich (2011,2014), Lesage, Navarrete, and De Neys (2013), Sirota, Juanchich, and Hagmayer (2014)). In particular, cognitive reflection is negatively related to conservatism bias, which refers to the inability of an individual to sufficiently update her prior beliefs in the face of new information (e.g., Phillips and Edwards (1966), Edwards (1968), Beach and Braun (1994)). We expect cognitive reflection to protect traders from conservatism bias and thereby lead them to actively use market orders as signals of other traders’ private information. Cognitive reflection should therefore help traders to better assess the value of an asset and in turn lead to higher trading profits.
To properly infer other traders’ private signals from market orders, traders also need to know how much information other traders hold. BQB show that one's ability to infer others’ intentions—referred to as “theory of mind” (ToM)—is a key to assessing the informational content of trades. Because traders who possess high ToM skills are more likely to detect the amount of information contained in market orders such as bids, asks, and prices, they typically have a better assessment of the precision of these market signals. We thus expect high ToM traders to have a more accurate assessment of the true value of an asset than low ToM traders and in turn higher trading profits.
In addition to predicting that fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM positively relate to earnings, we also conjecture a complementary relationship between ToM and cognitive reflection. Traders who have high cognitive reflection ability are able to avoid conservatism bias and thus update their assessment of asset value based on other traders’ market orders. ToM should be most relevant for these traders as this skill allows them to discern the precision of the signal offered by market orders. We also identify situations in which learning from market signals is of particular relevance. This is the case, for example, when a trader's private signal is imprecise because substantial information may be learned from market orders. In this case we conjecture that both cognitive reflection and ToM skills, which are key to inferring other traders’ private information, will be most relevant. Finally, we expect traders’ skills to be more relevant when there is a high degree of heterogeneity in these skills, in which case high-skill traders should be able to realize higher trading profits than low-skill traders.
Our primary analysis focuses on 2,839 profit outcomes for 167 traders who each trade in 17 experimental market periods. Using these market experiments, we find support for each of our conjectures. In particular, our results indicate that successful traders demonstrate a high level of fluid intelligence, an ability to avoid behavioral biases such as conservatism bias, and an ability to infer other traders’ intentions.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionIwe review the relevant prior literature. In SectionIIwe describe the experimental design. We develop our conjectures in SectionIIIand present our main results in SectionIV. Finally, in SectionVwe conclude.
article.I. Contributions to Prior Literature:
Our work connects several strands of the literature that identify fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM skills as predictors of trader behavior. Previous studies examine these three factors in isolation using different types of data and different measures of performance. An important contribution of our work is to jointly assess the predictive power of these three variables in a unified setting in which we use precise measures of trader performance and control for the flow of public and private information in the market as well as for a wealth of individual characteristics.
Fluid intelligence measures have been found to relate to stock market participation and successful investment decisions. Using a unique database of adult Finnish men, Grinblatt, Keloharju, and Linnainmaa (2011) study the relationship between market participation and IQ scores (as measured using the Finnish Armed Forces Intelligence Assessment). According to Dutton and Lynn (2013), the Finnish IQ test, which they refer to asPeruskoe, assigns an important weight to the Raven progressive matrices test (Raven (1941)).1The authors report that high-IQ Finnish men are more likely to participate in the stock market than those with low IQ scores. This finding is consistent with earlier studies suggesting a positive relationship between general cognitive ability and self-reported stock market participation (Kezdi and Willis (2003), Cole and Shastry (2009), Christelis, Jappelli, and Padula (2010), Benjamin, Brown, and Shapiro (2013)). Grinblatt, Keloharju, and Linnainmaa (2012) also show, using the same database of Finnish men, that the trades of high-IQ people outperform those of low-IQ people. These high-IQ individuals exhibit better market timing than their low-IQ counterparts and are more likely to buy winning stocks and sell losing stocks. These findings suggest that high-IQ people may be able to mitigate behavioral biases such as the well-documented disposition effect (e.g., Odean (1998), Chen et al. (2007)). However, the authors leave to further research analysis of the exact factors underlying the large returns of high-IQ traders. They note that “High-IQ investors may have better access to nonpublic information, may be better at processing information, or their greater immunity to behavioral biases may boost their returns” (p. 361). Our work shows that IQ explains trader performance even after controlling for traders’ private information and other individual characteristics. In addition, we show that cognitive reflection and ToM also help explain trader performance. These findings suggest that one's immunity to behavioral biases as measured by CRT scores is crucial to explaining trader performance, independent of a person's IQ.
A large literature in behavioral finance demonstrates that the heuristics and biases identified by cognitive psychologists (e.g., Tversky and Kahneman (1974)) can distort financial decisions (e.g., Shleifer (2000), Barber and Odean (2001), Thaler (2005), Shefrin (2007), Shiller (2015)). Research in cognitive science validates CRT for capturing a person's ability to avoid common behavioral biases (e.g., Oechssler, Roider, and Schmitz (2009), Toplak, West, and Stanovich (2011,2014)). In the experimental finance literature, CRT scores have been found to predict subjects’ earnings in asset markets with bubbles (Corgnet et al. (2014), Noussair, Tucker, and Xu (2014)). CRT questions are also very similar to some of the brainteasers that prospective traders are asked to solve during job interviews with Wall Street companies (Crack (2004), Zhou (2008)).2Thoma et al. (2015) show that a sample of 102 professional traders scored particularly high on the CRT and that CRT scores are positively correlated with years of experience and salary.3Of particular interest to our study is the negative relationship between cognitive reflection and conservatism bias, whereby individuals insufficiently update their prior beliefs (Oechssler, Roider, and Schmitz (2009). Hoppe and Kusterer (2011)). Conservatism bias has been used in the behavioral finance literature to help explain both under- and overreaction to news (e.g., Barberis, Shleifer, and Vishny (1998); see Shiller (2002) for a review). The authors model conservatism bias by assuming that traders believe earnings follow a mean-reverting process until sufficient positive or negative evidence accumulates. This implies that traders underreact to news in the short term, in line with momentum in stock markets (e.g., Cutler, Poterba, and Summers (1991), Bernard (1992), Jegadeesh and Titman (1993), Rouwenhorst (1997)). Baker and Stein (2004) develop a model in which the presence of traders who suffer from conservatism is essential to boost liquidity. Because conservative traders have limited information, an increase in liquidity in a stock is interpreted as a signal that a large proportion of uninformed traders are driving the evolution of prices, which can subsequently lead to low returns.
Our work contributes to the behavioral finance literature by demonstrating that cognitive reflection, which has been shown to be the best predictor of an individual's ability to avoid behavioral biases, significantly affects trader earnings. This finding adds to the literature by directly connecting trader performance to a specific cognitive skill.
Of course, thinking about prices and forecasting them are integral to successful trading, but these two steps alone leave out the actual placing of orders. Trading intuition concerns not only assessment of what is going on in the market and prediction of future prices, but also submission of the right orders. Our study only considers the first two facets; future work should shed light on the third. (BQB, p. 1721)
Our work addresses this third facet of successful trading by assessing the relationship between trader performance and individual characteristics such as ToM skills.
Other variables related to financial decision making and trader performance such as financial literacy, self-monitoring, and miscalibration are discussed in Internet Appendix Section III.A.
article.II. Design:
Our experimental asset market environment is similar to the experimental design of PS. The only notable departure from the original design is that our study uses a computerized rather than oral continuous double auction. We made this choice because computerized continuous double auction trading mechanisms are widely used in both actual stock market exchanges (Parsons et al. (2008)) and online prediction markets (www.predictit.org).6The PS design is suited to our purpose as it implements a rich environment that includes both public and private information. A trader's ability to update her belief about the value of an asset using both her private information and the information she infers from other traders’ orders is the key element of our study of the determinants of individual trader performance.7
We use the same parameters as in the PS study (Market 9, Treatment C) regarding possible asset values, endowments, and number of market replications. Each experimental session consisted of 17 five-minute market periods during which participants trade an experimental asset whose exact value was not known with certainty. This asset could take the value of 50, 240, or 490 francs with probability 0.35, 0.45, or 0.20, respectively. A franc was worth $0.001.
At the beginning of each market period, every trader was informed of a value the asset could not take. Since half of the traders were given one hint (e.g., “Not 50”) and the other half were given the other possible hint (e.g., “Not 240”), the aggregate information available to all traders in the market was complete. PS argue that in rational expectations equilibrium prices should reflect the true value of the asset (e.g., 490). In this paper, we focus on individuals’ trading behavior rather than on the aggregation of information at the market level.8
At the end of each market period, traders were informed about the asset's true value, their earnings for the given period, and their cumulative earnings across periods. Traders were not informed about other market participants’ earnings. Cash and shares of the asset were not carried over from one market period to the next. We use the same dividend realizations from Market 9 of PS in each session to facilitate comparisons across sessions.
We recruited a total of 204 participants from a subject pool of more than 1,500 students at a major Western U.S. university. We conducted a total of 17 sessions, each with 12 traders. In the 10 baseline sessions, traders were endowed with 1,200 francs in cash and four shares. To ensure that our results are not artifacts of this specific endowment structure, two sessions were run using the PS approach of endowing each subject with a 25,000-franc loan that had to be repaid at the end of each period and four shares. We added a third treatment to assess the robustness of our findings to higher stakes by doubling both the traders’ initial cash endowment (to 2,400 francs) and the value of shares (to 100, 480, and 980).9
At the beginning of each session subjects sat at their own workspace, read through the web-based instructions, and completed a comprehension quiz (see Internet Appendix Section VII). As part of the instructions, subjects also completed a training exercise regarding the probability of occurrence of each possible asset value (50, 240, or 490 francs) at the end of each market period. This training (see Internet Appendix Section VII) consisted of having subjects predict the outcome of a spinning wheel over 10 trials. Each correct prediction was rewarded 25 cents, and each incorrect answer incurred a 10-cent penalty. Upon completion of the comprehension quiz, subjects participated in a practice round for which they were not compensated. Average earnings (including a $7 show-up fee) for the 2.5-hour experiments were equal to $48.0 in our 10 baseline sessions, $33.5 in our two loan sessions, and $86.3 in our five high-stakes sessions. We summarize our experimental design in TableI.
As part of the experiment, participants answered questionnaires regarding various psychological and cognitive traits on two separate occasions.
At the end of each session, subjects answered demographic questions and completed a series of cognitive tests including the CRT and Raven test. The duration of the survey was 25 minutes. These tasks were computerized and the tests were not incentivized although subjects earned a $3 flat fee for completing the survey. The survey included tests for financial literacy and self-monitoring. In the five high-stakes sessions, we also included a Bayesian updating test taken from Charness and Levin (2009). In four of the five high-stakes sessions, we asked subjects to assess their session rank on the CRT (see Internet Appendix Section V for the corresponding analysis). We describe the tests used in the end-of-experiment survey in detail in Internet Appendix Section I.
One month after the original study, all of the subjects were invited to complete a one-hour survey as part of the laboratory policy of collecting information about subjects who are registered in the pool. A large majority of participants completed the one-hour survey (82%, or 167 out of 204).10Importantly, the subjects who completed the survey do not differ significantly from the subjects who did not complete the survey in terms of trader earnings (p-value = 0.16, Wilcoxon rank-sum test, WRS henceforth). The survey was computerized and, as is common practice in the literature, the tests were not incentivized although subjects earned a $15 flat fee. The survey captured our primary measures of trader performance, fluid intelligence, ability to avoid behavioral biases, and ability to infer others’ intentions.11
article.III. Conjectures:
In deriving our main conjectures, we posit that a trader's ability to learn an asset's true value using both her private information and market orders is key to her success. All else equal, a more informed trader will ultimately post more profitable orders, which will result in higher earnings. Thus, to ensure positive trading profits, a trader should attempt to discover an asset's true value. Similar reasoning is employed by Biais et al. (2005) when they argue that trader performance depends crucially on one's capacity to infer the true value of an asset in a winner's curse—a situation in which a trader's private signal along with the observed price of the asset may induce traders to incorrectly believe that the market price reflects the true value of the asset.
We postulate that learning the true value of an asset requires three fundamental attributes: fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM. To learn the true value of the asset, a trader needs to make statistical inference calculations based on available signals. In our setting, these available signals correspond to both private information given to traders at the beginning of each market period and publicly observed market orders. Traders learn the true value of the asset using Bayesian inference on these signals. These calculations require working memory capacity, which refers to the short-term holding and manipulation of information. Working memory is a central component of fluid intelligence (Conway, Kane, and Engle (2003)). Our data also show that fluid intelligence correlates positively and significantly with Bayesian updating skills (see Internet Appendix Section IV for details). We thus expect fluid intelligence to predict one's capacity to learn the true value of the asset, which is a key to engaging in profitable trading. In particular, we conjecture that fluid intelligence relates positively to trader earnings (Conjecture1).
Fluid intelligence, however, is not a sufficient condition for traders to learn the true value of an asset given market signals. Stanovich (2009) argues that fluid intelligence captures a specific set of cognitive skills and fails to assess one's propensity to deliberately engage in reasoning. Stanovich, West, and Toplak (2016) therefore distinguish between fluid intelligence, which is assessed using IQ tests (e.g., Raven (1941)), and cognitive reflection, which is assessed using the CRT. At the core of the distinction between fluid intelligence and cognitive reflection is the observation that one's capacity to engage in reasoning and refrain from providing automatic and thoughtless answers (see the CRT developed by Frederick (2005)) is vital to avoiding commonly observed heuristics and biases whereas fluid intelligence is not (e.g., Oechssler, Roider, and Schmitz (2009), Campitelli and Labollita (2010), Hoppe and Kusterer (2011), Toplak, West, and Stanovich (2011,2014), Lesage, Navarrete, and De Neys (2013), Sirota, Juanchich, and Hagmayer (2014)).12It follows that, in our market environment, computational skills are likely not sufficient to avoid behavioral biases and learn the true value of the asset. Traders who possess high fluid intelligence are able to make the computations necessary for statistical inference but may be subject to behavioral biases that lead them to ignore or misperceive the content or precision of the signals. Of particular interest to us is the commonly observed conservatism bias (e.g., Phillips and Edwards (1966), Edwards (1968), Beach and Braun (1994)), which is important to understanding a trader's failure to use market orders to update her prior beliefs. Eyster, Rabin, and Vayanos (2015) refer to traders who fail to use market prices as signals of other traders’ private information ascursedtraders. The presence of these traders is essential to capturing the winner's curse phenomenon (Rabin (2002)), which has been found to be pervasive in our trading environment (Biais et al. (2005)). Conservatism bias can also account for the fact that market prices in our experimental markets are typically consistent with the prior information equilibrium (Lintner (1969)) with traders making decisions based solely on their private information and failing to reflect on market orders (bids, asks, and prices) to uncover other traders’ information. People who demonstrate a high level of cognitive reflection have been found to not fall prey to conservatism bias (Oechssler, Roider, and Schmitz (2009), Hoppe and Kusterer (2011)). We replicate this finding with our data, as we show that two different measures of conservatism correlate negatively and significantly with CRT but not with Raven scores (see Internet Appendix Section IV for details). As such, we expect cognitive reflection to protect traders from conservatism bias in our market setting, and in turn lead them to use market orders as signals of other traders’ private information. Cognitive reflection thus helps traders better assess true asset value, which leads to higher trading profits (Conjecture1).
To accurately infer other traders’ private signals from market orders, traders also need to know how much information other traders hold. For example, the informational content of market orders varies with whether they are posted by insider traders or by uninformed traders. The capacity to detect insider trading has been shown to depend on ToM skills (BQB (2010)). It follows that traders who possess high ToM skills can accurately assess the precision of market signals such as bids, asks, and prices available in the market. We thus expect high ToM traders to learn true asset value with more precision than low ToM traders and in turn to earn higher trading profits (Conjecture1).
Conjecture 1.Fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM are positively related to earnings.

We also conjecture a complementarity between ToM and cognitive reflection. In particular, ToM should be most relevant for traders who actively use market orders to update their prior beliefs on the value of an asset. Because traders who are high in cognitive reflection are likely to avoid conservatism bias and thus actively use market signals to update their prior beliefs, we conjecture that ToM skills are most relevant for those traders who have a high level of cognitive reflection. Indeed, a trader who is high in fluid intelligence but suffers from conservatism bias will fail to use market orders as signals about other traders’ private information and thus not benefit from a better assessment of the precision of market order signals. We thus posit a positive complementarity between ToM skills and cognitive reflection whereas we expect no such complementarity between ToM and fluid intelligence (Conjecture2).
Conjecture 2.The positive effect of ToM on earnings is more pronounced for traders with high CRT scores.

Following Biais et al. (2005), we also identify situations in which learning accurately from market signals is of particular relevance. One such situation is when a trader receives an imprecise signal regarding the true value of an asset. In our experiment, this occurs when traders receive the hint “Not 240,” in which case the signal does not provide clear guidance regarding true asset value (50 or 490). By contrast, the hints “Not 50” and “Not 490” provide clearer guidance regarding the true value of the asset. In statistical terms, the signal “Not 240” is less precise than the signals “Not 50” and “Not 490.”13The less precise a trader's hint is, the more there is to learn from market orders about other traders’ private information. It follows that both cognitive reflection and ToM, which are key to learning the true value of the asset from market orders, should matter more when traders’ private information consists of imprecise signals of the true value of the asset (“Not 240”) than more precise signals (“Not 50” or “Not 490”) (Conjecture3).
Conjecture 3.The positive effect of cognitive reflection and ToM is more pronounced for traders who receive an imprecise hint (“Not 240”) than for those traders who receive a more precise hint (“Not 50” or “Not 490”).

Conjecture3represents a situation in which learning is important due to imprecise private signals. Another situation in which learning from market orders is important is the case of winner's curse traps as identified by Biais et al. (2005) in a market setting similar to ours. We analyze the case of winner's curse traps in detail in Internet Appendix Section II.B.
In markets that are populated entirely by high-skill traders, there is no opportunity for these traders to obtain above-average profits. We thus expect traders’ skills to be more relevant when there is heterogeneity in skill levels, in which case high-skill traders will be able to realize higher trading profits than low-skill traders (Conjecture4).
Conjecture 4.The effect of traders’ skills is more pronounced when there is heterogeneity in traders’ skill levels.

In deriving Conjectures1to4, we argue that the main driver of traders’ earnings is their capacity to learn the true value of an asset. In particular, traders who follow the strategy of buying below and selling above their belief about the true value of the asset will achieve higher earnings when their belief is more accurate. It follows that trader skills should affect not only their earnings but also their decisions to submit orders. In particular, increased skills should help traders obtain a more accurate estimate of true asset value, which should make them more likely to buy (bid) at a price that is below the true asset value and sell (ask) at a price which is above true value. To assess the extent to which a trader follows a strategy that is consistent with the true value of the asset, we say that an order (bid, ask, or trade) is potentially profitable when a bid or a purchase (an ask or a sale) is lower (higher) than or equal to the true value of the asset. We expect Conjectures1to4to hold when using the proportion of potentially profitable orders as the dependent variable in lieu of trader earnings.
Conjecture 5.We expect that the proportion of potentially profitable orders that traders engage in predicts their earnings. Moreover, we expect Conjectures 1 through 4 to hold when using the proportion of potentially profitable orders as the dependent variable in lieu of trader earnings.
article.IV. Experimental Results:
We analyze our experimental data for evidence with respect to Conjectures 1 through 5. Toward that end, we operationalize fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM using Raven, CRT, and ToM scores.
Internet Appendix Section VIII provides figures displaying the evolution of asset prices across the 17 market periods for each of the 17 sessions we conducted.
To investigate our conjectures, we use linear panel regressions with random effects in which each subject is treated as a cross-sectional observation (n= 167) and each market as a time-series observation (t= 17).14-16In each regression, we report robust standard errors clustered at the session level. The reason is that each experimental market is a zero-sum game in which an increase in one trader's earnings mechanically reduces other traders’ possible gains. It follows that traders’ earnings are correlated within a session, thus making the clustering of standard errors at the session level an appropriate methodological choice. Nevertheless, the qualitative nature of our findings is unchanged when using robust standard errors clustered at the individual level (see Internet Appendix Section III.G). We add treatment dummy variables that control for either the type of endowment given to subjects (Loan Dummy) or for the stakes (High-Stakes Dummy). In the regressions using trader earnings as the dependent variable, the coefficients onLoan Dummyshould be negative because subjects earned less on average in those sessions ($33.5) than in the baseline sessions ($48.0) in which they were given a $1.2 endowment each period. For the high-stakes sessions, we divide subjects’ earnings by two so that average earnings are in line with those in the baseline. High-stakes sessions closely resemble our baseline sessions when considering aggregate market variables (see Figure IA3 in Internet Appendix Section III.D). Price dynamics are essentially the same between the two treatments.17For example, the mean absolute deviation of average prices with respect to true asset value (the main measure of information aggregation used in PS) does not significantly differ between the baseline and high-stakes sessions (p-value = 0.27, WRS). In Internet Appendix Section III.D, we also report no significant effect of high-stakes sessions on our individual analysis of trader performance. It follows that increasing the stakes does not play a major role in the current design. Thus, in the main analysis, we pool the high-stakes sessions with the other sessions.
We also control for the actual value of the asset in a given market (Asset Value) and for the hint received by the subject in a given market period (“Not 50,” “Not 240,” or “Not 490”). We expect the regression coefficients onAsset Valueto be positive because subjects earned more in markets in which the value of the asset was higher. We control for gender using a dummy variable that takes the value one if the trader is a male and zero otherwise (Male Dummy). All individual characteristic measures are standardized to facilitate comparisons of the magnitudes of the coefficients. Our three predictor variables (Raven Score,CRT Score, andToM Score) correlate only moderately, so we choose to jointly use the three test scores as independent variables in our regression analyses.18A collinearity diagnostic indicates variance inflation factors close to one for Raven, CRT, and ToM in each of the regression analyses reported in the current paper. This suggests that collinearity is not a major concern.
We find that Raven, CRT, and ToM scores positively relate to trader earnings. In Figure1, we compare the earnings of traders in the bottom and top quartiles in terms of these variables.19We observe that Raven, CRT, and ToM scores have a positive effect on earnings. The earnings gap is the largest when comparing traders in the bottom quartile ($33.39) of Raven, CRT, and ToM scores with traders in the top quartile ($39.46). A trader who is in the top quartile of all three skills earns about the same as the top 25% of earners ($39.84), whereas a trader who is in the bottom quartile of all three skills earns less than the bottom 25% of earners ($35.65).
Trader earnings. This figure shows median trader earnings for participants in the bottom and top quartiles for Raven, CRT, and ToM scores. This figure also shows the bottom quartile, median, and top quartile for the whole distribution of participants’ earnings (see dashed horizontal lines).
†p-values are based on panel regressions with random effects and robust standard errors clustered at the session level where the dependent variable is market earnings and the independent variable is a dummy whose value is one if the trader is in the top quartile of Raven, CRT, ToM, or all three variables.
††In the first three comparisons, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile for a given skill (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets (17). In the last comparison, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile forallthree skills (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets (17). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Using panel regressions that control for market variables as well as numerous trader-specific characteristics, we show that, in line with Conjecture1, Raven, CRT, and ToM scores positively affect earnings (see columns (1) and (2) in TableII). In Internet Appendix Section IV, we also show that the positive effect of Raven and CRT scores on earnings relates, as conjectured, to traders’ ability to perform Bayesian calculations and avoid conservatism bias.20
We find support for Conjecture2by interacting ToM scores with both Raven and CRT scores. Indeed, the interaction terms for ToM and CRT are positive and significant (see columns (3) and (4) in TableII), suggesting that ToM matters most for traders with high CRT scores. In line with Conjecture2, we do not observe a significant complementary effect between ToM and Raven scores.
To test Conjecture3we create two subsets of our data based on whether traders received an imprecise hint (“Not 240”) or precise hint (“Not 50” or “Not 490”). We run regressions on these data sets using traders’ earnings as the dependent variable. We find the coefficients on bothToM ScoreandCRT Scoreare positive and significant when traders receive an imprecise hint, but insignificant when traders receive a precise hint (see columns (1) and (2) in TableIII). We run an additional regression on the entire sample including the dummy variable,Imprecise Dummy, which takes the value one for an imprecise hint and zero otherwise. This variable has a significant negative effect on earnings (see column (3) in TableIII). We also interacted this dummy variable with our three main predictors,Raven Score,CRT Score, andToM Score. Consistent with Conjecture3, the interaction betweenImprecise DummyandToM Scoreis both positive and significant (see column (3) in TableIII). Thus, we find evidence that ToM is the most consequential skill for a trader with an imprecise hint. This result holds when additional controls, including financial literacy, personality traits, and risk attitudes, are added to the regression (see Internet Appendix Section III.A).
To test Conjecture4, we require a measure of heterogeneity in traders’ skills. We define a skills index as the average of a trader's standardized Raven, CRT, and ToM scores. We then calculate the interquartile range (the difference between the 75th percentile and the 25th percentile) for each of the 17 sessions. We divide the sample between sessions that have a higher degree of heterogeneity in traders’ skills (i.e., larger interquartile range) than the median session and those that have a lower degree of heterogeneity than the median session. We define the dummy variableHigh Heterogeneity Dummyas taking the value one if the session's heterogeneity is greater than the median session and zero otherwise. We show that traders’ skills are more valuable in the case of high heterogeneity in skills than in the case of low heterogeneity in skills, as the coefficient onHigh Heterogeneity Dummyinteracted with the skills index is both significant and positive (see column (1) in TableIV).
Further, we show that traders with high CRT or ToM scores perform better in markets in which there is a high degree of heterogeneity in traders’ skills (see column (2) in TableIV), as the coefficients on the interactions betweenHigh Heterogeneity Dummyand the CRT and ToM scores are positive. The interaction term is statistically significant for ToM scores (p-value = 0.039) but not for CRT scores (p-value = 0.185). For Raven scores, we do not report a positive interaction effect with heterogeneity in skills in a given session. This may be due to the fact that fluid intelligence is a basic computational skill that is necessary to use one's private information adequately. Fluid intelligence is thus likely to be useful regardless of whether there exists a large number of unskilled traders in the market from which to extract additional earnings.
In line with the previous earnings results, we show that Raven, CRT, and ToM scores relate positively to the proportion of potentially profitable orders submitted by traders. Recall that an order (bid, ask, or trade) is defined as potentially profitable when a bid or a purchase (an ask or a sale) is lower (higher) than or equal to the true value of the asset. We illustrate our findings graphically in Figure2.
Proportion of potentially profitable orders. This figure shows median proportions of potentially profitable orders for participants in the bottom and top quartiles for Raven, CRT, and ToM scores. This figure also shows the bottom quartile, median, and top quartile for the whole distribution of the proportion of potentially profitable orders (see dashed horizontal lines).
†p-Values are based on panel regressions with random effects and robust standard errors clustered at the session level where the dependent variable is the proportion of potentially profitable orders and the independent variable is a dummy whose value is set to one if the trader is in the top quartile of Raven, CRT, ToM, or all three variables.
††In the first three comparisons, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile for a given skill (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets in which they submitted an order. In the last comparison, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile forallthree skills (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of market periods in which they submitted an order. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
In line with Conjecture5, we first find that a trader's proportion of potentially profitable orders relates positively and significantly to trader earnings (see Internet Appendix Section II.A, Table IAXI). We also find that, consistent with Conjecture1for earnings, all three trader skills (Raven, CRT, and ToM) affect the proportion of potentially profitable orders positively (see Internet Appendix Section II.A, Table IAXII). However, the coefficient fails to reach statistical significance for ToM scores. The fact that ToM scores positively impact earnings but not the submission of potentially profitable orders suggests that traders with high ToM may be less inclined to follow a fundamentalist strategy that consists of systematically buying the asset below and selling the asset above true (fundamental) value. This implies that ToM may be of even greater importance in market settings in which not following a fundamentalist trading strategy is profitable. In line with this observation, Hefti, Heinke, and Schneider (2016) show that ToM skills positively correlate with trader performance in markets prone to bubbles and crashes.
We do find support, however, for the positive interaction between ToM and cognitive reflection, as ToM scores interact significantly with CRT scores to positively influence the proportion of potentially profitable orders (see columns (3) and (4) in Internet Table IAXII). In line with Conjecture3for earnings, we find that CRT scores positively explain the proportion of potentially profitable orders for traders with an imprecise hint (see columns (1) and (3) in Internet Table IAXIII), while they do not significantly explain the proportion of potentially profitable orders for traders with a precise hint (see column (2) in Internet Table IAXIII). Consistent with Conjecture4for earnings, we find that our skills index as well as ToM scores significantly and positively explain the proportion of potentially profitable orders in markets with a high degree of trader heterogeneity. Indeed, the coefficients on the interactions betweenHigh Heterogeneity Dummyand the skills index and ToM variables are significant and positive (see Internet Table IAXIV). However, the coefficients on the interactions betweenHigh Heterogeneity Dummyand the CRT and Raven variables are not significant (see Internet Table IAXIV).
We provide multiple robustness checks in Internet Appendix Section III, none of which significantly affect the nature of our findings. In Internet Appendix Section III.A, we include controls for additional trader-specific characteristics such as financial literacy, risk attitudes, educational background (school attended and grades), and personality traits (big five personality traits and self-monitoring scale). In Internet Appendix Section III.B, we add session fixed effects. In Internet Appendix Section III.C, we define trader performance measures for the entire experiment abstracting from the panel structure of our data set, and we consider dynamics by allowing for an autoregressive structure of order one for the error term. In Internet Appendix Section III.D, we confirm that our high-stakes sessions are similar to the baseline sessions. In Internet Appendix Section III.E, we show that defining trader-specific variables as deviations from the session (cohort) averages does not alter our primary results. In Internet Appendix Section III.F, we replicate our main findings by performing our analyses of earnings and the proportion of potentially profitable orders using dummy variables for traders who scored in the top quartile of CRT, Raven, and ToM. Finally, in Internet Appendix VI, we provide a separate analysis of gender effects.
article.V. Conclusion:
Our work shows that fluid intelligence, cognitive reflection, and ToM skills drive trader performance in a market environment in which we control not only for the flow of information and the true value of the asset but also for commonly studied variables such as financial literacy, personality traits, and risk attitudes.
In our market environment, one's capacity to learn the true value of the asset using both private signals and market orders is key to engaging in profitable trading strategies. This holds due to the information structure of the market: private information is distributed among traders, which implies that market orders serve as public signals about other traders’ private information. In market settings where private information plays a lesser role, asset prices may move away from fundamentals systematically, making it optimal for traders to engage in speculative strategies rather than track fundamentals. Although these market settings may differ in terms of the optimal trading strategy to follow, an independent posterior study (Hefti, Heinke, and Schneider (2016)) confirms our main findings regarding the crucial role of analytical and ToM skills in market experiments in which bubbles are likely to emerge (Smith, Suchanek, and Williams (1988)) and traders are likely to engage in speculative activities.21
You don't need a rocket scientist. Investing is not a game where the 160 IQ guy beats the guy with the 130 IQ… Rationality is essential…
